data.name: Matěj Kovář
data.description: A favourite in the Czechia national team after the few days in the spring when he helped seal two playoff successes via penalty shootouts. Before then he had only saved one spot-kick in domestic football, but he excelled in the scrap for a World Cup spot like nobody else in the Czech squad. “It feels like a dream,” he said after the wins over the Republic of Ireland and Denmark. Kovar is a huge talent, calm by nature and mostly phlegmatic. As a teenager, he left a small Czech town to join Manchester United. He has since won three league titles in three different countries: Czechia, Germany and the Netherlands. Although he’s usually quiet and modest, he thrives in the big moments and coaches joke that he brings luck. “It wasn’t any great luck, I simply did my homework,” he said while the whole country was lauding him. Still, speculation persists over whether he will be the first-choice goalkeeper for the World Cup.

data.name: David Zima
data.description: Zima can come across as a rather unusual young man, especially in his public appearances. At first glance, you cannot tell what kind of day he’s having. After the first question, you still don’t know how the interview will go. Zima – meaning “winter” – is not a flashy star, despite the fact his career quickly took off as a teenager. At 19, with just 156 minutes in the domestic league, he was signed by Slavia Prague from Sigma Olomouc for more than €1m, a fee rarely seen in Czech football for such a player. The move did not faze him. “I have no reason to be nervous,” he said. “The pitch is always the same size. Opponents don’t scare me.” A year and a half later, he moved to Italy for four times that fee, joining Torino. In January 2024, he returned to Slavia Prague to restart his career and, at €4m, became the most expensive signing in Czech league history. Now a four-time domestic league champion, he is widely praised for his precise approach to defending, though critics still say he can be too laid-back, and prone to defensive lapses.

data.name: Tomáš Holeš
data.description: Fans of human anatomy should take a look at Tomas: the centre-back has what may well be the biggest calves at the tournament. He’s not particularly tall, but is pure muscle. He’s also one of the most experienced players in the Czechia squad and is a quiet leader at Slavia Prague, where he has won four league titles, the latest as captain. He comes from a region where cross-country skiing is more common than football but fell in love with the game competing with his two brothers. With no proper pitch in his village, he used to cycle three miles to training. Through consistency and hard work, Holes has gradually developed into a leader without ever acting like a superstar. Despite Czechia being a largely secular country, he is a devout evangelical Christian and says: “Faith is the most important thing for me, apart from my family. I often feel like guardian angels help me on the pitch.” Away from the pitch, Holes enjoys the quiet life with his two daughters, often going fishing and reading history books before bed.

data.name: Robin Hranáč
data.description: Hranac grew up in a small town in western Czechia, far from major academies – so when his talent became clear, that had to change. At 14, he travelled 60 miles to a boarding school in Plzen while his parents worked across the border in Germany. The sacrifices paid off. These days he has a calm and reserved exterior, and can appear shy, but that disguises the real fight and passion inside him. "Robin takes a lot of hits but never reacts or gets angry,” says his dad. “He has an extremely high pain threshold.” After just one impressive season in the Czech league he broke into the national team, marked Cristiano Ronaldo at Euro 2024, and earned an €8m move to Hoffenheim in 2024. Best deployed in the middle of a back three.

data.name: Vladimír Coufal
data.description: Long overlooked due to his slight build, Coufal was told at 17 by coaches at the Czech club Banik Ostrava that he would never make a living from football. He was quick and determined, but too small and easily outmuscled in duels. At that time, he hated football. “But I promised myself I’d prove them all wrong. I’m a bit of a madman who likes chasing his dreams,” he said. True recognition only came at 26, when he was signed by Slavia Prague and he went on to win three consecutive league titles before making a surprise move to the Premier League. “At first I thought they had the wrong name. Why would they sign a veteran?” he joked of his move to West Ham. In London he enjoyed a fairytale spell, culminating in winning the Europa Conference League. But after five years he found himself on the periphery with the Hammers. Again, though, he refused to give up. Now 33, he has rebuilt his career at Hoffenheim and is playing arguably his best football.

data.name: Štěpán Chaloupek
data.description: One of the breakout stars of the recent Czech league season, one of the country’s best out-and-out defenders and a champion with Slavia, Chaloupek’s performances were such that he attracted interest from Roma and Fiorentina in January. A year earlier, however, he wasn’t even in the starting lineup after a tough time with injury. He’s a hard-working, disciplined and reliable defender – a nightmare for attackers due to his uncompromising style. Off the pitch, he is quiet and reserved. Had football not worked out, he says he might have become a police officer. “I liked the idea of being fair, principled and standing up for the truth,” he says. Chaloupek was born with a cleft palate and underwent surgery at the age of three. “I was mocked by other kids because of it. It was hard, but it made me stronger.” He later considered plastic surgery as he still breathes through only one nostril.

data.name: Ladislav Krejčí
data.description: Krejci’s childhood dream of playing in the Premier League turned into something of a nightmare last season as Wolves endured defeat after defeat, suffering relegation to the Championship. The centre-back, however, quickly – and to the surprise of some – turned into one of Rob Edwards’ most valuable players. Over the past four years, his rise has been rapid. He won two league titles with Sparta Prague, became the most expensive signing in Girona’s history, before a loan to the Premier League last summer. Krejci follows a simple motto: ”If you feel you should do, decide or change something, don’t hesitate. No one else will do it for you.” He used to struggle with discipline and picked up too many yellow cards. Yet since March, he has been captain of the national team and led with distinction, scoring in the playoff games against the Republic of Ireland and Denmark. “I believed in myself because I know what I can do,” said the 27-year-old.

data.name: Vladimír Darida
data.description: An unassuming character, when Darida played for Freiburg from 2013 to 2015 he rode a scooter to training. He got rid of it when he moved to Berlin with Hertha, though. “It wasn’t really safe in Berlin traffic,” he said. “My father took it back to Czechia and I gave it to my sister.” It wasn’t an easy journey into professional football: he almost had to quit the game when he was 16 when his growth stopped, leaving him struggling to handle the physicality of the game. But, via hard work and a gluten-free diet, he developed the strength needed. He spent seven years at Hertha before moving to Aris in Greece in 2022, and returning to Czechia last year with Hradec Kralove. He brings huge experience, having made his international debut back in 2012, and captained the team at Euro 2020. After that tournament he retired from international football, saying: “I experienced beautiful moments in the national team, but now, I’d like to spend much more time with my family and my young son.” But he was tempted back in March this year, returning, aged 35, to help them in the World Cup qualification playoffs.

data.name: Adam Hložek
data.description: A real talent, who could make a big impact. Hlozek has played in Germany since 2022 when Bayer Leverkusen took note of his record at Sparta Prague: a senior debut at 16, scoring 40 goals and making 36 assists in 132 games, and winning the Czech Cup. He joined for an initial €13m, but stayed for only two years, having grown frustrated with a rotational role, moving to Hoffenheim for an initial €17m. Hugely versatile, he’s a threat out wide or through the middle. He’s a big fan of tattoos, with a giant lion on his left thigh. “It has a deep, personal meaning for me and gives me strength,” he said last year. “Leo is my star sign and I identify with the animal. The lion is known for its fighting mentality, exudes an aura and is the king of the wilderness. It took five and a half hours in the first sitting, then we took a break and after that it was another five and a half hours. But I'm happy that I got through it. I like it a lot."

data.name: Patrik Schick
data.description: Schick’s goalscoring gift was obvious from a very young age. Playing for Vestec, a village near Prague, he didn’t celebrate his goals, said coaches, but would instead just pick the ball up, take it to the centre circle and ask for the game to be resumed. When he missed a big chance, he would cry and had to be substituted. Aged eight he was inspired by watching Wayne Rooney play for Manchester United at Sparta Prague. He recalled: “I thought that I want to do this – never get a normal job, and earn a living like him.” It worked out well. He played a grand total of 36 league minutes in Sparta Prague’s first team before Sampdoria decided they had seen enough, paying €4m for the gangly 20-year-old. He signed for Roma a year later in a move that could have been worth up to a club record €42m, but it didn’t really work out there: he ended up on the wing, was loaned to Leipzig, then in 2020 was sold for €26.5m to Leverkusen – which is where everything clicked. In April this year, he scored his 100th goal for them in all competitions. He helped Czechia qualify for the first time in 20 years via the playoffs. He was 10 when they last played at a World Cup. “I vividly remember the games. The Czech fans have waited a long time for this.”

data.name: Jan Kuchta
data.description: An experienced forward who made his senior debut for Slavia Prague back in 2016, and now, a decade later, turns out for their rivals Sparta. Kuchta also had spells in Russia with Lokomotiv Moscow and Denmark with Midtjylland. Life with the international side hasn’t always been easy: in November 2023 he was one of three players – with Jakub Brabec and Vladimir Coufal – kicked out of the training camp for going clubbing two days before a Euro 2024 qualifier against Moldova. Aggressive and powerful on the pitch, he’s more nurturing off it, raising chickens, rabbits, ducks and geese at his family farm. When he moved to Moscow in 2022 and had to leave them behind he said: "Chickens are part of my life, I was used to going to them in the morning, then again after training, it cleared my head. It will take a while to adjust. I can’t take them there, it’s -17 degrees … The chickens will just have to wait until I am home, and I will pay a lot of attention to them again."

data.name: Lukáš Červ
data.description: A cousin of his teammate David Doudera, Cerv made his international debut two years ago, soon after joining Viktoria Plzen from Slovan Liberec, and was the only newcomer in the national side’s Euro 2024 squad. He wasn’t expecting the call when it came. “I didn’t really believe it at first,” he said after arriving at the team’s training camp. “I went on vacation, so when I got the call I had to ​​leave my family there and fly away. I left them there to enjoy the warmth and sunshine. And here we are in the rain and fog. But it's really incredible." Settling in as part of the national setup wasn’t too hard, though: “I know a lot of guys from the under-21s and I have Doudis [Doudera], my cousin, and the guys from Plzen here, so everything is fine. But I'm probably hanging out with Doudis most.” Two years on and he’s fully integrated, and an experienced, reliable option for this World Cup squad. His surname translates as “worm”.

data.name: Mojmír Chytil
data.description: A tall, experienced option up front, Chytil made quite a splash on his international debut in 2022 – scoring a 10-minute hat-trick in a friendly against the Faroe Islands. He’s known for being physical and fearless, a real handful, and could be an important weapon this summer. He spent most of his early career at Sigma Olomouc before 2023’s move to Slavia Prague where, again, he had a scoring debut, netting against Hradec Kralove on the opening day. The club’s manager, Jindrich Trpisovsky, described him well: “The first thing that comes to mind about Mojmir is the wider benefit he brings to the team: his fighting spirit, his annoyingness for defenders. He has very good ball coverage, perhaps the best back-to-goal game in the league. He is a team player, combative, with excellent physical qualities.”

data.name: David Jurásek
data.description: When Jurasek moved from Slavia Prague to Benfica for €14m three years ago, becoming one of the Czech league’s most expensive exports, it seemed Czech football would finally have a full-back at a top European club. His father even flew to Portugal by private jet for the signing ceremony, as Benfica set his release clause at a whopping €80m, an indication of the regard in which they held him. However, a serious ankle injury disrupted his progress, and he lost his place in the Benfica first team. Jurasek rebuilt his career during a loan spell at Hoffenheim, before returning to Slavia in January. “I just hope I don’t bring bad luck,” he confessed before helping them to the league title – the first title success of his career. With a strong left foot, excellent stamina and acceleration, a big physical presence and a great crossing ability, Jurasek has just about every attribute you would want from a left-back.

data.name: Pavel Šulc
data.description: Widely linked with a big move after this tournament – this summer is a real chance for Sulc to showcase his talents. Soon after being voted the best Czech player of 2025 he joined Lyon from Viktoria Plzen for an initial €7.5m, handed the formidable task of filling the void left behind by Rayan Cherki. But he shrugged off that pressure and stepped up with some superb displays, earning plenty of praise from supporters. The reception surprised him. “It’s been incredible,” he said in April. “Honestly, I didn't expect so much love from fans or that it would happen so quickly … It’s my first experience in one of the top five leagues in Europe, and the football in Czechia is simply much less demanding. The coach has been brilliant with me.” He’s happy to be versatile, too. “I'll always do my best, even if they put me as a centre-back!”

data.name: Jindřich Staněk
data.description: Stanek has never got anything for free in his career. He started out as an ice hockey player and was a promising forward, a talent he inherited from his father. But he chose to leave the ice in pursuit of a football career and went straight from Sparta to England, where he was a backup goalkeeper at Everton, facing shots from Romelu Lukaku, Kevin Mirallas and Samuel Eto’o in training. “I felt like I was in another world. I was taking a battering, but I didn’t want to give up,” he later recalled. He dreamed of following in the footsteps of his compatriot Petr Cech, but had to take the long way round. He even spent a season in the Czech third division to build his confidence. ”In hindsight, I feel like a cat with seven lives,” he said of his nomadic career. “That’s why when I go to the zoo, I spend most of my time with the tigers. I look for inspiration in them.” He eventually settled with Viktoria Plzen, where his career took off, leading to a move to Slavia Prague in 2024.

data.name: Lukáš Provod
data.description: An imposing 1.88m (6ft 2in) presence out wide, he is pacy as well. Provod rose through the ranks at his hometown club, Viktoria Plzen, then had a series of loans as he established himself, first at Banik Sokolov, then Ceske Budejovice and finally Slavia Prague, whom he joined on a permanent deal in 2020. “It feels unbelievable,” he said when he signed, “it feels like a beautiful story. It all happened really fast. It’s a great challenge for me, and I did not hesitate. Slavia is the biggest and most successful club in Czechia, so it was clear immediately.” He made his international debut the same year in a Nations League game against Slovakia. He missed Euro 2020 because of injury, but made the squad for 2024 and scored in the opener against Portugal. A versatile performer, he could also be called upon to do a job more centrally, or up front.

data.name: Michal Sadílek
data.description: At 16, Sadilek made a bold move. Instead of following his older brother Lukas through the academy system in his home country, he left for the Netherlands, joining PSV. Maybe the time apart did them good. “As kids we were together every day,” remembered Michal, “and sometimes like all little boys we got on each other's nerves, fought and the like.” But these days they couldn’t be closer. “I’m proud of my brother. We write to each other after every match, and when there's a chance, we talk about everything.” He moved to Twente in 2022 for three years, before returning home with Slavia in 2025. His international career hasn’t been without its ups and downs: he made his debut in 2021 but had to pull out of the Euro 2024 squad after injuring his leg when he fell off his tricycle at the training camp. The FA said the incident happened when he drove the three-wheeled bike through a pothole and crashed, but it did not criticise him. “The accident happened in the player's free time, which he was allowed to spend as he pleased. He did not violate team rules or other internal agreements."

data.name: Tomáš Chorý
data.description: A 1.98m (6ft 6in) powerhouse who isn’t afraid of getting stuck in. In January he embraced his bad-boy image, saying: “People don’t like me? That actually makes me feel good.” But in May things reached a tipping point: he was sent off for elbowing an opponent in the head in a chaotic Prague derby, his third red of the season, prompting Slavia’s furious chair, Jaroslav Tvrdik, to transfer-list him. “Tomas is clearly unable to control his behaviour,” said Tvrdik. “With this behaviour, he does not belong on the field.” The league’s disciplinary committee banned him for six games, saying the elbow was “not an accidental event … neither emotions nor the assertive style of play that Tomas Chory presents can be an excuse for such an action”. He has plenty of experience, and has spent his whole club career in Czechia apart from a loan spell in Belgium with Zulte Waregem. He made his international debut in 2023, scoring and assisting in a 3-0 Euro 2024 qualifying win over Moldova.

data.name: Jaroslav Zelený
data.description: One of the select few footballers who fans have forgiven for playing for both rival Prague clubs. An easy-going character, Zeleny is in no way a rebel or troublemaker, but he can be impatient, particularly when driving his car. “It annoys me when someone dawdles at the lights because they are on their phone,” he says. “That’s when I lean on the horn. Otherwise, I’m calm. People say you need to be ruthless in top football, but it’s not in my nature.” Zeleny is unlikely to change his nature now as he approaches his 34th birthday. He has never left the Czech league, making over 300 appearances since his debut for Hradec Kralove in 2010. Earlier in his career, he may have lacked the courage to move abroad; now he’s nearing the end of his time as an evergreen of Czech football. Fittingly, his surname translates to “green” in English.

data.name: David Douděra
data.description: Like his Slavia teammate Tomas Chory, Doudera had a lively Prague derby in May, with both shown red cards as the game descended into a mess of crowd violence. Doudera’s red was for abusing the referee; Slavia’s chair, Jaroslav Tvrdik, responded by transfer-listing him. The defender later apologised, writing online: "I'm sorry for the whole situation. To the fans, to the whole of Slavia, to Czech football. These things shouldn't happen in a civilised world.” He now has a chance to redeem himself this summer. He made his international debut in 2023, a year after his move to Slavia. He said soon afterwards: “My dad and I always say that my story is worth a book. But I don't want to brag about it, it's just that I have it in me. Each of us has a story and everyone got here somehow. I wasn't a great talent from a young age. I was never in the youth national teams … I'm proud of myself for what I've accomplished.” Teammate Lukas Cerv is his cousin.

data.name: Tomáš Souček
data.description: It wasn’t an easy season for Soucek as West Ham struggled, but he remains a fundamental part of Miroslav Koubek’s national side – a brilliantly energetic 1.93m (6ft 4in) force in midfield. Last year he was named Czech footballer of the year for the fourth time. He was brought up in Havlickuv Brod, a town with a population of 23,000 people, and comes from a sporting family: his dad a football coach and his mum a runner. “She runs half-marathons and marathons and sometimes when I was younger I went with her to the forest,” he says. “And sometimes when I was in pre-season I would go with her to run in the forest. I think I got my stamina from her.” Last year he published his autobiography, in which he revealed he had considered retiring early during what were “two years of horror” spent with insomnia and depression. He improved with medical help, and said his trademark “helicopter” goal celebration rose from the experience: “From match to match, I focused mainly on not sleeping, which actually disturbed me doubly. And that’s why the helicopter was created, my goal celebration. I start to spin slightly and enjoy the feeling of taking off. I like it when fans call me a helicopter. It’s a symbol of what I’ve done, what I’ve overcome, and where I am. It expresses my inner feeling that I can fight.”

data.name: Lukáš Horníček
data.description: Hornicek has spent roughly a third of his life in Portugal, where he moved aged 17. He was based in a boarding school 1,500 miles from home and was homesick, but never gave up his dream of a career in football. “That decision took away part of the youth I could have spent with friends, but I followed my dream. Even if I could turn back time, I wouldn’t change it,” he says. Nicknamed “Horny Czech“ in Portugal because of the difficulty of pronouncing his surname, he’s now a rising star in Czech goalkeeping. He started in Braga’s youth team and spent two years as a backup, before a spectacular breakthrough last season. He kept a clean sheet every other match and became known for saving penalties in the Europa League. Boasting high levels of concentration, he has improved his distribution and work on the ball in Portugal and has even been linked with Real Madrid of late. “It’s a shame my grandfather didn’t live to see [this]. He was my biggest supporter,” he says.

data.name: Alexandr Sojka
data.description: Raised in a footballing family. Sojka’s dad, Jindrich, was chair of SK Klatovy for more than 20 years, and his older brother plays for them now. Alexandr has gone the furthest in the family, though. He returned to his first club, Viktoria Plzen, this year when they activated a buy-back clause, re-signing him from Hradec Kralove. “We decided to activate the option," said the club’s sporting director, Martin Vozábal. "The goal is to increase competition in midfield and he performed very well during the autumn.” He has proved himself as a technically gifted, versatile performer. His international inclusion was an overwhelming moment for his dad. "The whole family lives for it,” Jindrich told iSport. “The only pity is that his grandfather, a big fan of Plzen since childhood, did not live to see it. Sasa [Alexandr] is finally showing what is in him. People in this region live for football and they appreciate that a boy from Klatovy can reach such a level."

data.name: Hugo Sochůrek
data.description: The baby of the squad, Sochurek was still 17 when the final selection was announced. A product of Sparta’s academy, he made his club debut last year, and his international debut in a warm-up game against Kosovo in May. But his cool, fearless approach convinced the coach, Miroslav Koubek, he was ready. After his first-team breakthrough at Sparta, their coach, Brian Priske, said: “He plays like he's over 30. The first-team players immediately took him in and when they saw what he could do with the ball.” Sochurek’s youth coach Pavel Drsek told Eurofotbal.cz in May: “He's made a huge improvement in the last year, he’s grown bigger, he has strong thighs, it's hard to get him off the ball. He’s not a typical Czech midfielder, he likes the ball, his first thought is to go forward … And he's a great guy, a pure soul, but quiet. Once I handed him the captain's armband before a match and he immediately told me that he could not be the captain. He is not a classic leader, but he is clearly a driving force.”

data.name: Denis Višinský
data.description: A promising young winger who knew what he wanted from the moment he first kicked a ball. “I first played in the village where I was born, for AFK Horin, at the age of three,” he recalled. “Then we moved and I started playing for Melnik. When I was six, we played a tournament somewhere, I don't know where exactly, and Slavia Prague scouted me there. So I went to Slavia for a trial and joined them when I was six.” He made rapid progress, and last year signed for Viktoria Plzen from Slovan Liberec. Viktoria’s sporting director, Daniel Kolar, called him “an extraordinary talent, which he confirmed in the Liberec jersey. Despite his still young age, he has already played almost 80 league matches. We have a clear development plan with him and we intend to push his potential even further.” Technically gifted, he has pace and an eye for goal – a useful option this summer. Models himself on Kevin De Bruyne.
